question:
B-2. In a round-robin tournament with \( n \) players \( P_{1}, P_{2}, \cdots, P_{n} \) (where \( n>1 \) ), each player plays one game with each of the other players and the rules are such that no ties can occur. Let \( \omega_{r} \) and \( l_{r} \) be the number of games won and lost, respectively, by \( P_{r} \). Show that
\[
\sum_{r=1}^{n} w_{r}^{2}=\sum_{r=1}^{n} l_{r}^{2}
\]

descriptive_long.question:
B-2. In a round-robin tournament with \( playercount \) players \( firstplayer, secondplayer, \cdots, lastplayer \) (where \( playercount>1 \) ), each player plays one game with each of the other players and the rules are such that no ties can occur. Let \( totalwins \) and \( gameslost \) be the number of games won and lost, respectively, by \( P_{r} \). Show that
\[
\sum_{r=1}^{playercount} gameswon^{2}=\sum_{r=1}^{playercount} gameslost^{2}
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
B-2. In a round-robin tournament with \( whileloop \) players \( watermelon, blacksmith, \cdots, trebuchet \) (where \( whileloop>1 \) ), each player plays one game with each of the other players and the rules are such that no ties can occur. Let \( chandelier_{r} \) and \( butterfly_{r} \) be the number of games won and lost, respectively, by \( P_{r} \). Show that
\[
\sum_{r=1}^{whileloop} riverside_{r}^{2}=\sum_{r=1}^{whileloop} butterfly_{r}^{2}
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
B-2. In a round-robin tournament with \( soloplayer \) players \( spectatorone, spectatortwo, \cdots, spectatorlast \) (where \( soloplayer>1 \) ), each player plays one game with each of the other players and the rules are such that no ties can occur. Let \( losescore_{r} \) and \( winsscore_{r} \) be the number of games won and lost, respectively, by \( P_{r} \). Show that
\[
\sum_{r=1}^{soloplayer} losescore_{r}^{2}=\sum_{r=1}^{soloplayer} winsscore_{r}^{2}
\]

garbled_string.question:
B-2. In a round-robin tournament with \( gfdkalwm \) players \( rqmfndca, ptlevoxh, \cdots, smyxeruv \) (where \( gfdkalwm>1 \) ), each player plays one game with each of the other players and the rules are such that no ties can occur. Let \( hjgrksla \) and \( ksdjqjzp \) be the number of games won and lost, respectively, by \( P_{r} \). Show that
\[
\sum_{r=1}^{gfdkalwm} qzxwvtnp^{2}=\sum_{r=1}^{gfdkalwm} ksdjqjzp^{2}
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer $n\ge 2$.  
In a (single) round-robin tournament --- every unordered pair of distinct players meets exactly once and every game has a winner and a loser --- let  

$\bullet$ $w_r$ and $l_r$ be, respectively, the numbers of games won and lost by player $P_r$;  

$\bullet$ $d_r=w_r-l_r$ be the score-differential of $P_r$.

Answer the following four questions.

(a)  Prove that $\displaystyle\sum_{r=1}^{n}d_r=0$.

(b)  For every non-empty subset $T\subseteq\{1,2,\dots ,n\}$ with $t=|T|$ players show that  
\[
\sum_{r\in T}d_r\;\le\;t\,(n-t).
\tag{1}
\]

(c)  An integer $n$-tuple $(d_1,\dots ,d_n)$ is the score-differential vector of \emph{some} round-robin tournament on $n$ players if and only if simultaneously  

\quad(i)   $\displaystyle\sum_{r=1}^{n}d_r=0$;  

\quad(ii)  Inequality (1) holds for every non-empty subset $T\subseteq\{1,\dots ,n\}$;  

\quad(iii) Every coordinate satisfies the parity restriction  
\[
d_r\equiv n-1\pmod 2\qquad(r=1,\dots ,n).
\]

(d)  Denote  
\[
S(n)=\sum_{r=1}^{n}w_r^{2}
\quad\bigl(\text{equivalently }S(n)=\sum_{r=1}^{n}l_r^{2}\bigr).
\]
Among all round-robin tournaments on $n$ players determine the exact minimum and maximum of $S(n)$ and describe \emph{all} tournaments at which each extremum is attained.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write $E(T,\overline T)$ for the collection of edges with one endpoint in $T$ and the other in its complement $\overline T$.

------------------------------------------------
(a)  Each game supplies exactly one win and one loss, so   
\[
\sum_{r=1}^{n}w_r=\binom{n}{2}=\sum_{r=1}^{n}l_r
\Longrightarrow 
\sum_{r=1}^{n}d_r=\sum_{r=1}^{n}(w_r-l_r)=0.
\qquad\square
\]

------------------------------------------------
(b)  Fix $T\subseteq[n]$ with $|T|=t$.  
Exactly $t(n-t)$ games are played across the cut $E(T,\overline T)$.  
Define  
\[
\operatorname{out}(T)=\sum_{r\in T}\#\{\text{wins of }P_r\text{ versus }\overline T\},\qquad
\operatorname{in}(T)=\sum_{r\in T}\#\{\text{losses of }P_r\text{ to }\overline T\}.
\]
Then $\operatorname{out}(T)+\operatorname{in}(T)=t(n-t)$ and  
\[
\sum_{r\in T}d_r
=\sum_{r\in T}(w_r-l_r)
=\operatorname{out}(T)-\operatorname{in}(T)
=t(n-t)-2\,\operatorname{in}(T)\le t(n-t).
\]
Thus (1) holds. $\square$

------------------------------------------------
(c)  Necessity and sufficiency of (i)-(iii).

Necessity.  Part (a) yields (i) and part (b) yields (ii); (iii) is immediate from  
$w_r+l_r=n-1\Longrightarrow d_r\equiv n-1\pmod 2$.

Sufficiency.  Assume an integer vector $\mathbf d=(d_1,\dots ,d_n)$ satisfies (i)-(iii) and define  
\[
s_r=\frac12\!\bigl((n-1)+d_r\bigr)\qquad(r=1,\dots ,n).
\]
Because of (iii) each $s_r$ is an integer with $0\le s_r\le n-1$, and by (i)
\[
\sum_{r=1}^{n}s_r=\frac12\sum_{r=1}^{n}(n-1+d_r)=\binom{n}{2}.
\]
Hence $\mathbf s=(s_1,\dots ,s_n)$ is a viable candidate for the out-degree sequence of a tournament.

Landau's classical criterion (stated in non-decreasing order) asserts:

Let $s_{(1)}\le\dots\le s_{(n)}$ be the increasing rearrangement of the $s_r$.
An integer sequence $(s_r)$ with the above bounds and sum is the score sequence of a tournament if and only if
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{k}s_{(i)}\;\ge\;\binom{k}{2}\qquad(k=1,\dots ,n).
\tag{2}
\]

We verify (2).  
For $k\in\{1,\dots ,n\}$ let $T_k$ be the set of indices of the $k$ \emph{largest} coordinates of $\mathbf s$.  
Because $s_r=\frac12[(n-1)+d_r]$, inequality (1) applied to the complement $\overline{T}_k$ gives
\[
\sum_{r\in T_k}d_r
=-\!\sum_{r\in \overline{T}_k}d_r
\;\ge\;-k(n-k),
\]
whence
\[
\sum_{r\in T_k}s_r
=\frac12\!\Bigl(k(n-1)+\sum_{r\in T_k}d_r\Bigr)
\;\ge\;\frac12\!\bigl(k(n-1)-k(n-k)\bigr)
=\binom{k}{2}.
\tag{3}
\]

Let $U_k=[n]\setminus T_{n-k}$; $U_k$ consists of the $k$ \emph{smallest} $s_r$.  
Exactly the same computation yields
\[
\sum_{r\in U_k}s_r\;\ge\;\binom{k}{2}.
\tag{4}
\]
Relation (4) is precisely (2) for the $k$ smallest coordinates, while (3) implies (2) for the $k$ largest ones (replace $k$ by $n-k$).  
Hence (2) holds for every $k$, so by Landau the multiset $\{s_1,\dots ,s_n\}$ is realised by some tournament; its score-differential vector is exactly $\mathbf d$. $\square$



------------------------------------------------
(d)  Extremal values of $S(n)=\sum_{r=1}^{n}w_r^{2}$.

Write  
\[
Q(\mathbf d)=\sum_{r=1}^{n}d_r^{2},\qquad
S(n)=\sum_{r=1}^{n}w_r^{2}.
\]
With $w_r=\frac12[(n-1)+d_r]$ and $\sum d_r=0$ one obtains
\[
S(n)=\frac14\sum_{r=1}^{n}\bigl((n-1)+d_r\bigr)^{2}
     =\frac{n}{4}(n-1)^{2}+\frac14\,Q(\mathbf d).
\tag{5}
\]
Thus maximising/minimising $S(n)$ is equivalent to maximising/minimising the quadratic form $Q(\mathbf d)$ over the feasible set  
\[
\mathcal D=\{\mathbf d\in\mathbb Z^{n}\text{ satisfying (i)-(iii)}\}.
\]


Maximum of $S(n)$.

Put $s_r=\frac12[(n-1)+d_r]$.  Because
\[
Q(\mathbf d)=4\sum_{r=1}^{n}\!\Bigl(s_r-\tfrac{n-1}{2}\Bigr)^{2}
           =4\sum_{r=1}^{n}s_r^{2}-n(n-1)^{2},
\]
maximising $Q$ is tantamount to maximising $\sum s_r^{2}$.

Majorisation argument.  
Let $\mathbf s=(s_1,\dots ,s_n)$ be the score sequence of a tournament and let $s_{(1)}\le\dots\le s_{(n)}$ be its increasing rearrangement.  
The Landau inequalities (2) together with their complements read
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{k}s_{(i)}\ge\binom{k}{2}\quad\text{and}\quad
\sum_{i=n-k+1}^{n}s_{(i)}\le\binom{n}{2}-\binom{k}{2}\qquad(k=1,\dots ,n).
\tag{6}
\]
The canonical multiset  
\[
\mathbf t=(0,1,2,\dots ,n-1)
\]
satisfies the equalities in (6).  Therefore $\mathbf t$ majorises every other score multiset, and Karamata's inequality gives  
$\sum s_r^{2}\le\sum t_r^{2}$, with equality only if $\{s_r\}=\{t_r\}$.

Hence the maximum of $\sum s_r^{2}$ and thus of $Q(\mathbf d)$ is attained exactly for the multiset $\{0,1,\dots ,n-1\}$.  
This score sequence is realised by any \emph{transitive} (acyclic) tournament, i.e. one obtained by totally ordering the vertices and orienting every edge forward.  Translating back,
\[
d_r=n-1-2(r-1)\qquad(r=1,\dots ,n),
\]
up to permutation.  Direct calculation yields
\[
Q_{\max}=\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}(n-1-2k)^{2}=\frac{n\bigl(n^{2}-1\bigr)}{3},
\qquad
S_{\max}(n)=\frac14\!\Bigl(n(n-1)^{2}+Q_{\max}\Bigr)
           =\frac{n(n-1)(2n-1)}{6}.
\]
Because of the preceding majorisation argument equality in $S\le S_{\max}(n)$ implies $\{s_r\}=\{0,1,\dots ,n-1\}$, hence the tournament is transitive.  Thus the maximum is realised \emph{only} by transitive tournaments.


Minimum of $S(n)$.

Because $\sum d_r=0$, the quadratic $Q(\mathbf d)$ is minimised when the coordinates of $\mathbf d$ are as balanced as the parity restriction allows.

* Odd $n=2m+1$.  
Parity forces every $d_r$ to be even.  Choosing a regular tournament (each player wins and loses $m$ games) yields $\mathbf d=\mathbf0$ and
$Q_{\min}=0$.  
Any non-zero feasible vector has at least one coordinate $\lvert d_r\rvert\ge2$, giving $Q\ge4$; hence regular tournaments (and only those) achieve the minimum.

* Even $n=2m$.  
Now every $d_r$ is odd, so $\lvert d_r\rvert\ge1$.  The least possible quadratic sum is obtained when \emph{exactly} $m$ coordinates equal $+1$ and the remaining $m$ equal $-1$, giving $Q_{\min}=m+m=2m=n$.

Existence of such tournaments.  
Label the vertices $0,1,\dots ,2m-1$ and perform all computations modulo $2m$.

Step 1 - orient edges of \emph{length} $1,2,\dots ,m-1$.  
For $i\ne j$ with $1\le (j-i)\bmod 2m\le m-1$ direct the edge $\{i,j\}$ from $i$ to $j$.  
This yields a digraph in which every unordered pair whose distance is not $m$ is oriented exactly once.

Step 2 - orient the $m$ antipodal edges of length $m$.  
For each $i\in\{0,1,\dots ,m-1\}$ orient the edge $\{i,i+m\}$ \emph{from} $i$ \emph{to} $i+m$.  

The two steps together orient every unordered pair exactly once, hence produce a tournament.  Let us count wins.

* Vertices $i<m$.  
From Step 1 they beat the $m-1$ vertices at distances $1,2,\dots ,m-1$, and from Step 2 they additionally beat $i+m$.  
Thus $w_i=m$ and $d_i=+1$.

* Vertices $i\ge m$.  
Step 1 grants them $m-1$ wins (against the vertices at backward distances $1,2,\dots ,m-1$), while Step 2 gives a \emph{loss} against $i-m$.  
Hence $w_i=m-1$ and $d_i=-1$.

Consequently the constructed tournament realises the differential vector
\[
(\underbrace{1,\dots ,1}_{m},\underbrace{-1,\dots ,-1}_{m}),
\]
so $Q=n$ and $S(n)$ attains its minimum.

Minimality.  Let $\mathbf d$ be any feasible vector with $Q(\mathbf d)<n+2$.  
Because every $d_r$ is odd, $\lvert d_r\rvert\ge1$, so $Q(\mathbf d)\ge n$.  
If some $\lvert d_r\rvert\ge3$, then  
$Q(\mathbf d)\ge 3^{2}+(n-1)\cdot 1^{2}=n+8>n$, impossible.  
Hence every $\lvert d_r\rvert=1$.  
Finally $\sum d_r=0$ forces exactly $m$ entries $+1$ and $m$ entries $-1$.  
Therefore $Q_{\min}=n$ and the above pattern is the \emph{only} one that achieves it.

Combining with (5),
\[
S_{\min}(n)=
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{n}{4}(n-1)^{2}, & n\ \text{odd},\\[10pt]
\dfrac{n}{4}(n-1)^{2}+\dfrac{n}{4}, & n\ \text{even}.
\end{cases}
\]
For odd $n$ equality occurs exactly at regular tournaments; for even $n$ at precisely the tournaments whose differential vector consists of $m$ entries $+1$ and $m$ entries $-1$.


Summary of extrema.

\[
\boxed{\;
S_{\max}(n)=\frac{n(n-1)(2n-1)}{6}\text{ --- realised exactly by the transitive tournaments;}
\qquad
S_{\min}(n)=
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{n}{4}(n-1)^{2}, & n\ \text{odd},\\[10pt]
\dfrac{n}{4}(n-1)^{2}+\dfrac{n}{4}, & n\ \text{even},
\end{cases}
}
\]
with equality in the second formula iff the tournament is regular (odd $n$) or has differential vector $\bigl(\underbrace{1,\dots ,1}_{m},\underbrace{-1,\dots ,-1}_{m}\bigr)$ (even $n=2m$). $\square$

original_kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer $n\ge 2$.  
In a (single) round-robin tournament --- every unordered pair of distinct players meets exactly once and every game has a winner and a loser --- let  

$\bullet$ $w_r$ and $l_r$ be, respectively, the numbers of games won and lost by player $P_r$;  

$\bullet$ $d_r=w_r-l_r$ be the score-differential of $P_r$.

Answer the following four questions.

(a)  Prove that $\displaystyle\sum_{r=1}^{n}d_r=0$.

(b)  For every non-empty subset $T\subseteq\{1,2,\dots ,n\}$ with $t=|T|$ players show that  
\[
\sum_{r\in T}d_r\;\le\;t\,(n-t).
\tag{1}
\]

(c)  An integer $n$-tuple $(d_1,\dots ,d_n)$ is the score-differential vector of \emph{some} round-robin tournament on $n$ players if and only if simultaneously  

\quad(i)   $\displaystyle\sum_{r=1}^{n}d_r=0$;  

\quad(ii)  Inequality (1) holds for every non-empty subset $T\subseteq\{1,\dots ,n\}$;  

\quad(iii) Every coordinate satisfies the parity restriction  
\[
d_r\equiv n-1\pmod 2\qquad(r=1,\dots ,n).
\]

(d)  Denote  
\[
S(n)=\sum_{r=1}^{n}w_r^{2}
\quad\bigl(\text{equivalently }S(n)=\sum_{r=1}^{n}l_r^{2}\bigr).
\]
Among all round-robin tournaments on $n$ players determine the exact minimum and maximum of $S(n)$ and describe \emph{all} tournaments at which each extremum is attained.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write $E(T,\overline T)$ for the collection of edges with one endpoint in $T$ and the other in its complement $\overline T$.

------------------------------------------------
(a)  Each game supplies exactly one win and one loss, so   
\[
\sum_{r=1}^{n}w_r=\binom{n}{2}=\sum_{r=1}^{n}l_r
\Longrightarrow 
\sum_{r=1}^{n}d_r=\sum_{r=1}^{n}(w_r-l_r)=0.
\qquad\square
\]

------------------------------------------------
(b)  Fix $T\subseteq[n]$ with $|T|=t$.  
Exactly $t(n-t)$ games are played across the cut $E(T,\overline T)$.  
Define  
\[
\operatorname{out}(T)=\sum_{r\in T}\#\{\text{wins of }P_r\text{ versus }\overline T\},\qquad
\operatorname{in}(T)=\sum_{r\in T}\#\{\text{losses of }P_r\text{ to }\overline T\}.
\]
Then $\operatorname{out}(T)+\operatorname{in}(T)=t(n-t)$ and  
\[
\sum_{r\in T}d_r
=\sum_{r\in T}(w_r-l_r)
=\operatorname{out}(T)-\operatorname{in}(T)
=t(n-t)-2\,\operatorname{in}(T)\le t(n-t).
\]
Thus (1) holds. $\square$

------------------------------------------------
(c)  Necessity and sufficiency of (i)-(iii).

Necessity.  Part (a) yields (i) and part (b) yields (ii); (iii) is immediate from  
$w_r+l_r=n-1\Longrightarrow d_r\equiv n-1\pmod 2$.

Sufficiency.  Assume an integer vector $\mathbf d=(d_1,\dots ,d_n)$ satisfies (i)-(iii) and define  
\[
s_r=\frac12\!\bigl((n-1)+d_r\bigr)\qquad(r=1,\dots ,n).
\]
Because of (iii) each $s_r$ is an integer with $0\le s_r\le n-1$, and by (i)
\[
\sum_{r=1}^{n}s_r=\frac12\sum_{r=1}^{n}(n-1+d_r)=\binom{n}{2}.
\]
Hence $\mathbf s=(s_1,\dots ,s_n)$ is a viable candidate for the out-degree sequence of a tournament.

Landau's classical criterion (stated in non-decreasing order) asserts:

Let $s_{(1)}\le\dots\le s_{(n)}$ be the increasing rearrangement of the $s_r$.
An integer sequence $(s_r)$ with the above bounds and sum is the score sequence of a tournament if and only if
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{k}s_{(i)}\;\ge\;\binom{k}{2}\qquad(k=1,\dots ,n).
\tag{2}
\]

We verify (2).  
For $k\in\{1,\dots ,n\}$ let $T_k$ be the set of indices of the $k$ \emph{largest} coordinates of $\mathbf s$.  
Because $s_r=\frac12[(n-1)+d_r]$, inequality (1) applied to the complement $\overline{T}_k$ gives
\[
\sum_{r\in T_k}d_r
=-\!\sum_{r\in \overline{T}_k}d_r
\;\ge\;-k(n-k),
\]
whence
\[
\sum_{r\in T_k}s_r
=\frac12\!\Bigl(k(n-1)+\sum_{r\in T_k}d_r\Bigr)
\;\ge\;\frac12\!\bigl(k(n-1)-k(n-k)\bigr)
=\binom{k}{2}.
\tag{3}
\]

Let $U_k=[n]\setminus T_{n-k}$; $U_k$ consists of the $k$ \emph{smallest} $s_r$.  
Exactly the same computation yields
\[
\sum_{r\in U_k}s_r\;\ge\;\binom{k}{2}.
\tag{4}
\]
Relation (4) is precisely (2) for the $k$ smallest coordinates, while (3) implies (2) for the $k$ largest ones (replace $k$ by $n-k$).  
Hence (2) holds for every $k$, so by Landau the multiset $\{s_1,\dots ,s_n\}$ is realised by some tournament; its score-differential vector is exactly $\mathbf d$. $\square$



------------------------------------------------
(d)  Extremal values of $S(n)=\sum_{r=1}^{n}w_r^{2}$.

Write  
\[
Q(\mathbf d)=\sum_{r=1}^{n}d_r^{2},\qquad
S(n)=\sum_{r=1}^{n}w_r^{2}.
\]
With $w_r=\frac12[(n-1)+d_r]$ and $\sum d_r=0$ one obtains
\[
S(n)=\frac14\sum_{r=1}^{n}\bigl((n-1)+d_r\bigr)^{2}
     =\frac{n}{4}(n-1)^{2}+\frac14\,Q(\mathbf d).
\tag{5}
\]
Thus maximising/minimising $S(n)$ is equivalent to maximising/minimising the quadratic form $Q(\mathbf d)$ over the feasible set  
\[
\mathcal D=\{\mathbf d\in\mathbb Z^{n}\text{ satisfying (i)-(iii)}\}.
\]


Maximum of $S(n)$.

Put $s_r=\frac12[(n-1)+d_r]$.  Because
\[
Q(\mathbf d)=4\sum_{r=1}^{n}\!\Bigl(s_r-\tfrac{n-1}{2}\Bigr)^{2}
           =4\sum_{r=1}^{n}s_r^{2}-n(n-1)^{2},
\]
maximising $Q$ is tantamount to maximising $\sum s_r^{2}$.

Majorisation argument.  
Let $\mathbf s=(s_1,\dots ,s_n)$ be the score sequence of a tournament and let $s_{(1)}\le\dots\le s_{(n)}$ be its increasing rearrangement.  
The Landau inequalities (2) together with their complements read
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{k}s_{(i)}\ge\binom{k}{2}\quad\text{and}\quad
\sum_{i=n-k+1}^{n}s_{(i)}\le\binom{n}{2}-\binom{k}{2}\qquad(k=1,\dots ,n).
\tag{6}
\]
The canonical multiset  
\[
\mathbf t=(0,1,2,\dots ,n-1)
\]
satisfies the equalities in (6).  Therefore $\mathbf t$ majorises every other score multiset, and Karamata's inequality gives  
$\sum s_r^{2}\le\sum t_r^{2}$, with equality only if $\{s_r\}=\{t_r\}$.

Hence the maximum of $\sum s_r^{2}$ and thus of $Q(\mathbf d)$ is attained exactly for the multiset $\{0,1,\dots ,n-1\}$.  
This score sequence is realised by any \emph{transitive} (acyclic) tournament, i.e. one obtained by totally ordering the vertices and orienting every edge forward.  Translating back,
\[
d_r=n-1-2(r-1)\qquad(r=1,\dots ,n),
\]
up to permutation.  Direct calculation yields
\[
Q_{\max}=\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}(n-1-2k)^{2}=\frac{n\bigl(n^{2}-1\bigr)}{3},
\qquad
S_{\max}(n)=\frac14\!\Bigl(n(n-1)^{2}+Q_{\max}\Bigr)
           =\frac{n(n-1)(2n-1)}{6}.
\]
Because of the preceding majorisation argument equality in $S\le S_{\max}(n)$ implies $\{s_r\}=\{0,1,\dots ,n-1\}$, hence the tournament is transitive.  Thus the maximum is realised \emph{only} by transitive tournaments.


Minimum of $S(n)$.

Because $\sum d_r=0$, the quadratic $Q(\mathbf d)$ is minimised when the coordinates of $\mathbf d$ are as balanced as the parity restriction allows.

* Odd $n=2m+1$.  
Parity forces every $d_r$ to be even.  Choosing a regular tournament (each player wins and loses $m$ games) yields $\mathbf d=\mathbf0$ and
$Q_{\min}=0$.  
Any non-zero feasible vector has at least one coordinate $\lvert d_r\rvert\ge2$, giving $Q\ge4$; hence regular tournaments (and only those) achieve the minimum.

* Even $n=2m$.  
Now every $d_r$ is odd, so $\lvert d_r\rvert\ge1$.  The least possible quadratic sum is obtained when \emph{exactly} $m$ coordinates equal $+1$ and the remaining $m$ equal $-1$, giving $Q_{\min}=m+m=2m=n$.

Existence of such tournaments.  
Label the vertices $0,1,\dots ,2m-1$ and perform all computations modulo $2m$.

Step 1 - orient edges of \emph{length} $1,2,\dots ,m-1$.  
For $i\ne j$ with $1\le (j-i)\bmod 2m\le m-1$ direct the edge $\{i,j\}$ from $i$ to $j$.  
This yields a digraph in which every unordered pair whose distance is not $m$ is oriented exactly once.

Step 2 - orient the $m$ antipodal edges of length $m$.  
For each $i\in\{0,1,\dots ,m-1\}$ orient the edge $\{i,i+m\}$ \emph{from} $i$ \emph{to} $i+m$.  

The two steps together orient every unordered pair exactly once, hence produce a tournament.  Let us count wins.

* Vertices $i<m$.  
From Step 1 they beat the $m-1$ vertices at distances $1,2,\dots ,m-1$, and from Step 2 they additionally beat $i+m$.  
Thus $w_i=m$ and $d_i=+1$.

* Vertices $i\ge m$.  
Step 1 grants them $m-1$ wins (against the vertices at backward distances $1,2,\dots ,m-1$), while Step 2 gives a \emph{loss} against $i-m$.  
Hence $w_i=m-1$ and $d_i=-1$.

Consequently the constructed tournament realises the differential vector
\[
(\underbrace{1,\dots ,1}_{m},\underbrace{-1,\dots ,-1}_{m}),
\]
so $Q=n$ and $S(n)$ attains its minimum.

Minimality.  Let $\mathbf d$ be any feasible vector with $Q(\mathbf d)<n+2$.  
Because every $d_r$ is odd, $\lvert d_r\rvert\ge1$, so $Q(\mathbf d)\ge n$.  
If some $\lvert d_r\rvert\ge3$, then  
$Q(\mathbf d)\ge 3^{2}+(n-1)\cdot 1^{2}=n+8>n$, impossible.  
Hence every $\lvert d_r\rvert=1$.  
Finally $\sum d_r=0$ forces exactly $m$ entries $+1$ and $m$ entries $-1$.  
Therefore $Q_{\min}=n$ and the above pattern is the \emph{only} one that achieves it.

Combining with (5),
\[
S_{\min}(n)=
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{n}{4}(n-1)^{2}, & n\ \text{odd},\\[10pt]
\dfrac{n}{4}(n-1)^{2}+\dfrac{n}{4}, & n\ \text{even}.
\end{cases}
\]
For odd $n$ equality occurs exactly at regular tournaments; for even $n$ at precisely the tournaments whose differential vector consists of $m$ entries $+1$ and $m$ entries $-1$.


Summary of extrema.

\[
\boxed{\;
S_{\max}(n)=\frac{n(n-1)(2n-1)}{6}\text{ --- realised exactly by the transitive tournaments;}
\qquad
S_{\min}(n)=
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{n}{4}(n-1)^{2}, & n\ \text{odd},\\[10pt]
\dfrac{n}{4}(n-1)^{2}+\dfrac{n}{4}, & n\ \text{even},
\end{cases}
}
\]
with equality in the second formula iff the tournament is regular (odd $n$) or has differential vector $\bigl(\underbrace{1,\dots ,1}_{m},\underbrace{-1,\dots ,-1}_{m}\bigr)$ (even $n=2m$). $\square$
